entities.name: apabhramśa
entities.cite: That deviation is apabhramsa, the 'fallen-away' form

entities.name: saṃskāra
entities.cite: samskara-hina, lacking the refinement grammar names

entities.name: viśiṣṭārtha
entities.cite: the fallen form settles on the distinguished meaning perfectly well

entities.name: prayukti
entities.cite: A speaker intends the correct gauh, 'cow'

entities.name: sadhu
entities.cite: the sadhu form it deviates from

axioms.name: samskara-hina
axioms.cite: samskara-hina, lacking the refinement grammar names (1.131's sabda-samskara, here in its technical sense)

axioms.name: nivesinam
axioms.cite: visistartha-nivesinam — the fallen form *settles on the distinguished meaning perfectly well*; the hearer gets the cow

axioms.name: intention-relative
axioms.cite: prayuyuksite ('when one intends to employ') marks the definition as intention-relative: the same phonetic form is apabhramsa only against a meant sadhu target

axioms.name: asrita
axioms.cite: the apabhramsa conveys, but conveys *through* the sadhu form it deviates from (as 1.152 will say), like a mispronounced name that reaches its bearer only because the right name is recoverable through it

denials.name: determined-by-success
denials.cite: Corruption is thus defined against intention and norm, not against communicative success

claims.name: intention-relative
claims.cite: when 'gauh' is what one intends to employ

rejected_readings.label: ignore_norm_criterion
rejected_readings.rendering: Since corrupt forms successfully communicate meaning, there is no difference between them and correct forms.
rejected_readings.why: Ignores the explicit doctrine: corruption is defined by norm and intention, not by communicative success. The axioms establish that apabhramsa both lacks samskara AND settles on meaning as independent features. Success does not erase the norm-violation.

rejected_readings.label: elide_intention_dependence
rejected_readings.rendering: The form 'gavi' is simply a variant pronunciation of 'gauh', with neither more correct than the other.
rejected_readings.why: Erases the intention-relative definition captured in the axioms. The contested point makes explicit: the same phonetic form is apabhramsa only against a meant sadhu target. This reading treats gavi and gauh as symmetric variants, missing that apabhramsa-status depends on what the speaker intends to say.

rejected_readings.label: deny_communicative_power
rejected_readings.rendering: Corrupted utterances like 'gavi' fail to reach their hearers or leave them confused about the meaning.
rejected_readings.why: Contradicts the nivesinam axiom: the fallen form settles on the distinguished meaning perfectly well; the hearer gets the meaning. Apabhramsa is communicatively successful by definition, even while being corrupt (norm-violating). These are independent properties in the Bhartṛhari framework.

notes: Verse 1.147 introduces apabhramsa (linguistic corruption) within a normative frame: corruption is relative to intention and standard form, not to communicative outcome. The contract captures four core axioms: apabhramsa as samskara-hina (lacking refinement), nivesinam (settling on distinguished meaning), intention-relative (definition depends on intended form), and asrita (conveyed through the sadhu form). The denial encodes what the verse explicitly rejects: defining corruption by success. The three rejected readings target common misinterpretations: treating success as a criterion, erasing intention-dependence, and denying communicative power. Together with 1.153-154, this verse establishes the edge-case status of forms native to speakers who intend nothing else.
